Source_Species.reason: SmJAZ8 is studied in Danshen, i.e., Salvia miltiorrhiza, throughout the paper.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The study organism is explicitly Salvia miltiorrhiza.

Applied_Species.reason: Functional assays refer to SmJAZ8 from Danshen/Salvia miltiorrhiza, including transient assays and interaction studies.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species context is Salvia miltiorrhiza; heterologous Nicotiana benthamiana was only used as an assay system.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: SmJAZ8 is discussed as a repressor affecting tanshinone biosynthesis via SmWRKY33.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The abstract states tanshinones are major bioactive diterpenes, a terpenoid class.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper states tanshinones accumulate in the periderm and SmWRKY33 is highest in periderm, but SmJAZ8 itself was not directly localized to root periderm in this study.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The abstract states tanshinones are major bioactive diterpenes widely used to treat cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: SmJAZ8 is explicitly a JAZ repressor; the Jas motif and EAR motif are directly discussed as functionally important in Figures 4E and 5C.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: SmJAZ8 protein degradation was tested under MeJA treatment and MG132 inhibition in Figure 5E.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The Results report Y2H, LCI, pull-down, Co-IP, dual-LUC, EMSA-based interference, and MeJA-induced degradation assays for SmJAZ8.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Figure 5B explicitly uses SmCPS1, SmKSL1, SmCYP76AH3, SmCYP71D373 and Sm2-ODD14 promoters in dual-LUC assays with SmJAZ8/SmWRKY33.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: This study supports SmJAZ8 acting as a JA-signalling repressor and shows its protein level decreases after MeJA treatment (Figure 5E).

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are directly supported: interaction with SmWRKY33 through the Jas motif (Figure 4E), repression of SmWRKY33 activation on five promoters (Figure 5B), reduction of DNA binding plus relief by MeJA-triggered degradation (Figure 5D-F).

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The paper supports SmJAZ8 as a repressor of tanshinone biosynthesis through inhibition of SmWRKY33, and MeJA-induced degradation relieves this repression.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Direct interaction with SmWRKY33 is supported by Y2H, LCI, pull-down, and Co-IP.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper shows the EAR motif is required for strong repression, the Jas motif is required for interaction, and MeJA triggers 26S proteasome-dependent degradation blocked by MG132.

Omics_Data.reason: Although RNA-seq dataset PRJCA038900 is reported for MeJA-treated hairy roots, SmJAZ8 is not presented as an omics-derived core finding in this paper.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The Discussion generally suggests molecular targets for metabolic engineering, but this specific engineering proposal for SmJAZ8 knockout/editing or combination strategies is not directly stated.

corrections.old_value: RNA-seq of MeJA-treated hairy roots, CNCB PRJCA038900
corrections.reason: Although RNA-seq dataset PRJCA038900 is reported for MeJA-treated hairy roots, SmJAZ8 is not presented as an omics-derived core finding in this paper.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: EVM0014685 is explicitly identified and then designated as SmWRKY33 in Results section 2.1.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The species studied is Salvia miltiorrhiza.

Applied_Species.reason: Stable transgenic Danshen plants and hairy roots were used to test function; Nicotiana benthamiana was used only for transient assays.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied biological context for metabolite effects is Salvia miltiorrhiza.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: SmWRKY33 was directly shown to regulate tanshinone accumulation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper explicitly links periderm-specific tanshinone accumulation with SmWRKY33 highest expression in periderm.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The abstract states tanshinones are major bioactive diterpenes used for cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases.

Regulator_Type.reason: SmWRKY33 is explicitly a WRKY transcription factor.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results state it is Group I WRKY with two WRKY domains, SP cluster, D domain, proline-rich N-terminus, and identify a C-terminal activation domain spanning aa 438-527.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Confocal microscopy with AtHY5 marker showed SmWRKY33 is nuclear localized.

Regulation_Mode.reason: SmWRKY33 is shown to be a transcriptional activator in yeast and dual-LUC assays.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: The paper directly demonstrates control of tanshinone biosynthesis.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: Dual-LUC, Y1H, and EMSA support direct activation/binding to SmCPS1, SmKSL1, SmCYP76AH3, SmCYP71D373 and Sm2-ODD14.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: Direct activation of the five listed promoters via W-box binding is shown; Figure 2 also shows indirect upregulation of additional biosynthetic genes including SmGGPPS1, SmCYP76AH1, SmCYP76AK1 and SmCYP71D375 in OE plants.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: The study presents SmWRKY33 as a key positive JA-responsive regulator of tanshinone biosynthesis affecting multiple pathway steps and final metabolites.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: MeJA induces SmWRKY33; SmJAZ8 represses its activity; SmMPK3 phosphorylates and enhances its activity.

Protein_Complex_Involvement.reason: Results support an inhibitory SmJAZ8-SmWRKY33 interaction and direct interaction with SmMPK3 kinase.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Interaction with SmJAZ8 and SmMPK3 is strongly supported; SmJAZ7 and SmJAZ3 interactions are also reported, though SmJAZ3 is weaker and from Y2H/LCI context.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are reported for SmWRKY33: overexpression, SRDX repression, dual-LUC, Y1H, EMSA, Y2H, LCI, pull-down, Co-IP, and metabolite profiling.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: MeJA treatment is central to its induction and regulation.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: The gene is stated to be highly/root preferentially expressed, with highest transcript levels in periderm.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results explicitly state JA-induced, root-enriched, highest in periderm and also detected in xylem by FISH.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four summarized points are directly supported by Results Figures 1-6.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Overexpression increases DHT, Tan I, CT and Tan IIA, while SRDX repression decreases them.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Figure 6 and Figure S14 support reduced DNA binding/transactivation for 5A, enhanced DNA binding for 5D, and no effect on localization or interactions.

Omics_Data.reason: SmWRKY33 was identified from RNA-seq of MeJA-treated hairy roots, and the raw data accession PRJCA038900 is explicitly provided.

Applied_Promoters.reason: SmWRKY33 overexpression used CaMV35S in stable transgenics and pSuper in transient assays, as stated in Results and Methods.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The abstract and Discussion explicitly describe the findings as providing molecular targets/promising targets for metabolic engineering to enhance tanshinone production.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The study organism is Salvia miltiorrhiza.

Applied_Species.reason: Functional tests were performed in transgenic Salvia miltiorrhiza hairy roots, with transient assays in Nicotiana benthamiana.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The metabolite and pathway effects are shown in Salvia miltiorrhiza.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: SmMPK3 overexpression was directly shown to increase tanshinone accumulation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Tanshinones are diterpenoid/terpenoid compounds in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper directly measures tanshinones in Danshen hairy roots; periderm enrichment is discussed for tissue context, though not directly measured for SmMPK3 metabolite accumulation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The abstract states tanshinones are major bioactive diterpenes used for cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases.

Regulator_Type.reason: SmMPK3 is explicitly described as a mitogen-activated protein kinase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results state SmMPK3 is a mitogen-activated protein kinase highly homologous to Arabidopsis MPK3.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Figure S10 is cited as showing SmMPK3 localizes to both nucleus and plasma membrane.

Regulation_Mode.reason: The paper shows SmMPK3 activates SmWRKY33 function through phosphorylation.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: SmMPK3 is presented as a positive regulator of JA-responsive tanshinone biosynthesis.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: SmWRKY33 is the direct protein target shown to interact with and be phosphorylated by SmMPK3.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: SmMPK3 enhances SmWRKY33-mediated activation of SmCPS1 in dual-LUC, and OE hairy roots upregulate SmGGPPS1, SmCPS1, SmKSL1, SmCYP76AH3, SmCYP71D373, SmCYP71D375 and SmWRKY33.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: The paper directly supports SmMPK3 as a positive JA-responsive kinase increasing tanshinone biosynthesis via SmWRKY33.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: SmMPK3 expression is significantly induced by MeJA.

Protein_Complex_Involvement.reason: Direct interaction with SmWRKY33 is supported by Y2H, pull-down, LCI and Co-IP.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: SmWRKY33 is directly shown to interact with SmMPK3.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are reported in Results/Methods, including co-expression analysis, RT-qPCR, Y2H, LCI, pull-down, Co-IP, in vitro kinase assay, LC-MS/MS, in vivo phosphorylation, dual-LUC, EMSA, hairy root overexpression and metabolite profiling.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: MeJA treatment is directly tested and induces SmMPK3.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results state SmMPK3 is induced by MeJA and enriched in xylem and periderm.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results explicitly report induction at 0.5 h after MeJA and transcript enrichment in xylem and periderm.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four points are directly supported by Sections 2.7-2.9, including major phosphosites T53, S78 and S90 and effects on metabolites.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: SmMPK3 positively regulates tanshinone accumulation through activation of SmWRKY33, as shown by phosphorylation and OE hairy root metabolite data.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results explicitly note an unexpected reduction of 2-ODD14 expression in SmMPK3-OE hairy roots despite increased tanshinones.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper shows interaction requires SmWRKY33 N-terminus and SmMPK3 C-terminus, and that SmWRKY33-5A responds only slightly in transactivation assays.

Omics_Data.reason: SmMPK3 emerged from co-expression analysis using the RNA-seq DEG set, and PRJCA038900 is the accession for the RNA-seq dataset.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state SmMPK3 was cloned into pK7WG2D for overexpression and into pSuper1300-GFP for transient assays.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper generally suggests molecular targets for metabolic engineering, but this specific statement about combining SmMPK3 with SmWRKY33 activation is not explicitly stated.
